videoTitle: The Thylacine: Hunted, Filmed, and Gone
description:
The thylacine looked like a striped dog, but it was something far older and stranger: the last modern member of a unique family of meat-eating marsupials.

This documentary follows the evidence from its ancient range across Australia and New Guinea to its final refuge in Tasmania. It examines the bounty system, the other pressures that may have deepened the decline, and the new museum research that corrected the story of the last known animal.

The final thylacine died at Hobart Zoo on 7 September 1936. Protection had begun only 59 days earlier.

Chapters:
00:00 The animal caught on film
00:30 A marsupial, not a tiger
01:00 A lost mainland range
01:31 Built unlike a dog
02:02 Life after dark
02:33 Conflict with settlers
03:04 A price on every animal
03:36 More than one pressure
04:08 The final years
04:42 Evidence, not rumour
05:14 A warning that remains
05:45 Next: Steller's Sea Cow

intro.title: The Thylacine
intro.subtitle: Thylacinus cynocephalus
intro.narration: We can still watch the thylacine move. Old film shows a striped animal pacing behind wire, alive within living memory. Yet the cameras came near the end. People had feared it, hunted it, and paid rewards for its body. When protection finally arrived, only fifty-nine days remained.

scenes.title: Not a Tiger
scenes.subtitle: The last of a unique family
scenes.narration: The thylacine was not a tiger, a wolf, or a dog. It was a marsupial, a mammal whose young grew inside a pouch. It belonged to its own family. By modern times, every close relative was already gone. The striped hunter in Tasmania was the final living branch.
scenes.imagePrompt: Cinematic wildlife documentary reconstruction of a scientifically plausible adult thylacine with a lean long-backed dog-like body, sandy yellow-brown short coat, about fifteen dark brown stripes across the lower back and rump, stiff tapering tail aligned with the spine, long narrow head, rounded upright ears and slim legs; full-body animal standing alert in open Tasmanian woodland, three-quarter eye-level view, thylacine centered slightly left with all key features visible, soft early morning light through pale eucalyptus trunks, photorealistic, realistic anatomy, historically appropriate natural environment, subtle film grain, restrained natural color grading, high detail, no text, no watermark, no logo, no collar, no modern objects, no blood, 16:9 widescreen composition
scenes.factNote: Thylacinus cynocephalus was the only member of Thylacinidae to survive into modern times.

scenes.title: A Range Lost
scenes.subtitle: Australia, New Guinea, and Tasmania
scenes.narration: Long before European settlement, thylacines lived across mainland Australia and New Guinea as well as Tasmania. The fossil record places their mainland loss at about three thousand two hundred years ago. Why they vanished there is still debated. Dingoes, changes in human activity, and climate may all have played a part.
scenes.imagePrompt: Cinematic wildlife documentary reconstruction of a scientifically plausible adult thylacine with a lean long-backed dog-like body, sandy yellow-brown short coat, about fifteen dark brown stripes across the lower back and rump, stiff tapering tail aligned with the spine, long narrow head, rounded upright ears and slim legs; animal crossing a broad ancient Australian plain with open woodland, rocky shelter and distant red escarpment, wide elevated camera view, thylacine small but clear in the lower left moving toward open country, warm late afternoon light, no dingo present, photorealistic, realistic anatomy, prehistoric natural environment, subtle film grain, restrained natural color grading, high detail, no text, no watermark, no logo, no collar, no modern objects, no blood, 16:9 widescreen composition
scenes.factNote: Reliable fossil dates estimate mainland extinction at about 3,227 years before present; the cause remains unresolved.

scenes.title: Built Unlike a Dog
scenes.subtitle: A lean hunter with a stiff tail
scenes.narration: At first glance, it looked like a long dog. The details were different. Its tail formed a stiff line from the back. Dark bands crossed the rear half of its body. Adults could reach about one point eight metres from nose to tail, but new research suggests the average animal was much lighter than old estimates claimed.
scenes.imagePrompt: Cinematic wildlife documentary reconstruction of a scientifically plausible adult thylacine with a lean long-backed dog-like body, sandy yellow-brown short coat, about fifteen dark brown stripes across the lower back and rump, stiff tapering tail aligned with the spine, long narrow head, rounded upright ears and slim legs; precise side profile walking across a simple Tasmanian grassland clearing, low eye-level camera, animal filling most of the frame with spine, tail, ears, feet and stripe pattern clearly visible, neutral overcast light suited to anatomy, photorealistic, realistic proportions, historically appropriate natural environment, subtle film grain, restrained natural color grading, high detail, no text, no watermark, no logo, no collar, no modern objects, no blood, 16:9 widescreen composition
scenes.factNote: A 2020 study estimated a mixed-sex adult mean mass near 16.7 kg, well below the older 29.5 kg estimate.

scenes.title: Life After Dark
scenes.subtitle: A shy hunter in open country
scenes.narration: Most reports describe a shy animal active at night or near dusk. It likely hunted alone or in pairs, using open forest, wet ground, and grassland. Wallabies, smaller mammals, and birds were part of its diet. Much more is uncertain. No scientist ever completed a modern field study while the species was alive.
scenes.imagePrompt: Cinematic wildlife documentary reconstruction of a scientifically plausible adult thylacine with a lean long-backed dog-like body, sandy yellow-brown short coat, about fifteen dark brown stripes across the lower back and rump, stiff tapering tail aligned with the spine, long narrow head, rounded upright ears and slim legs; thylacine moving quietly through a Tasmanian grassland at blue hour while a small wallaby stands far ahead and unharmed, ground-level tracking view, animal on the right with nose low and ears forward, moonlit wetland and dry forest mosaic behind, tension without attack, photorealistic, realistic anatomy, historically appropriate natural environment, subtle film grain, restrained natural color grading, high detail, no text, no watermark, no logo, no collar, no modern objects, no blood, 16:9 widescreen composition
scenes.factNote: Behaviour is reconstructed from old reports, captive records and anatomy; detailed hunting claims remain uncertain.

scenes.title: Conflict Arrives
scenes.subtitle: Sheep, fear, and a false enemy
scenes.narration: European farms spread across the same open country the thylacine used. Sheep arrived in Tasmania in the eighteen twenties. Losses were blamed on the striped predator, even when wild dogs and poor care were also responsible. A rare native hunter became an easy enemy, and fear soon turned into organised killing.
scenes.imagePrompt: Cinematic wildlife documentary reconstruction of a scientifically plausible adult thylacine with a lean long-backed dog-like body, sandy yellow-brown short coat, about fifteen dark brown stripes across the lower back and rump, stiff tapering tail aligned with the spine, long narrow head, rounded upright ears and slim legs; living thylacine paused outside a rough 1830s Tasmanian sheep fence at dusk, sheep safe and distant near a small colonial farm hut, wide camera view from the grass, animal in the left foreground watching but not attacking, storm light and long shadows, conflict implied without violence, photorealistic, realistic anatomy, historically appropriate setting, subtle film grain, restrained natural color grading, high detail, no text, no watermark, no logo, no collar, no modern objects, no blood, 16:9 widescreen composition
scenes.factNote: The scale of thylacine attacks on sheep was likely exaggerated, but the belief drove persecution.

scenes.title: A Price on Every Animal
scenes.subtitle: Tasmanian bounty, 1888–1909
scenes.narration: Private rewards began decades earlier. Then, in eighteen eighty-eight, the Tasmanian Government paid one pound for an adult and ten shillings for a young animal. The scheme lasted until nineteen oh nine. More than two thousand one hundred eighty bounties were paid. Each payment marked another animal removed from a shrinking population.
scenes.imagePrompt: Cinematic wildlife documentary reconstruction of a scientifically plausible adult thylacine with a lean long-backed dog-like body, sandy yellow-brown short coat, about fifteen dark brown stripes across the lower back and rump, stiff tapering tail aligned with the spine, long narrow head, rounded upright ears and slim legs; living animal half-hidden in Tasmanian scrub in the foreground while two distant 1890s hunters ride along a ridge with empty hands, long-lens camera view, thylacine on the lower right and unaware of the men, cold grey dawn, persecution implied without traps, injury or death, photorealistic, realistic anatomy, historically appropriate clothing and landscape, subtle film grain, restrained natural color grading, high detail, no readable text, no watermark, no logo, no collar, no modern objects, no blood, 16:9 widescreen composition
scenes.factNote: The government scheme paid £1 per adult and 10 shillings per juvenile; 2,184 claims were recorded.

scenes.title: The Pressures Close In
scenes.subtitle: Killing was not the only threat
scenes.narration: The bounty record does not explain every detail, but direct killing was the clearest pressure. Farms changed habitat and prey. Dogs may have competed with thylacines. Disease has also been suggested, but the evidence is weak. One major study found that known human pressures could explain the extinction without adding an epidemic.
scenes.imagePrompt: Cinematic wildlife documentary reconstruction of a scientifically plausible adult thylacine with a lean long-backed dog-like body, sandy yellow-brown short coat, about fifteen dark brown stripes across the lower back and rump, stiff tapering tail aligned with the spine, long narrow head, rounded upright ears and slim legs; solitary animal at the edge of fragmented early twentieth-century Tasmanian habitat, cleared pasture and fence on one side, shrinking dry forest and wetland on the other, distant domestic dog silhouette behind a fence, high wide camera view, thylacine small in the center, muted winter light, multiple pressures suggested without confrontation, photorealistic, realistic anatomy, historically appropriate setting, subtle film grain, restrained natural color grading, high detail, no text, no watermark, no logo, no collar on thylacine, no modern objects, no blood, 16:9 widescreen composition
scenes.factNote: Habitat change, reduced prey, dogs and disease remain debated; disease is not required to explain the modelled decline.

scenes.title: The Final Years
scenes.subtitle: Protection came 59 days too late
scenes.narration: The last known wild shooting was in nineteen thirty. Older accounts said the final zoo animal arrived in nineteen thirty-three. New museum work changed that story. It identifies the last animal as an older female caught in nineteen thirty-six. She died at Hobart Zoo on September seventh, just fifty-nine days after legal protection began.
scenes.imagePrompt: Cinematic wildlife documentary reconstruction of a scientifically plausible old adult female thylacine with a lean long-backed dog-like body, sandy yellow-brown short coat, about fifteen dark brown stripes across the lower back and rump, stiff tapering tail aligned with the spine, long narrow head, rounded upright ears and slim legs; animal standing quietly inside the historically accurate 1936 Hobart Zoo enclosure, eye-level view through soft out-of-focus wire, female on the right looking toward a dark shelter, cold late-winter evening light, respectful and restrained mood, no distress or death shown, photorealistic, realistic anatomy, historically appropriate enclosure, subtle film grain, restrained natural color grading, high detail, no text, no watermark, no logo, no collar, no modern objects, no blood, 16:9 widescreen composition
scenes.factNote: Current research identifies an old female caught in May 1936 as the last individual; the name “Benjamin” is not supported.

scenes.title: Evidence, Not Rumour
scenes.subtitle: The verified record ends in 1936
scenes.narration: Reports of living thylacines continued for decades. Searches crossed remote parts of Tasmania. None produced clear proof. What remains is strong physical evidence: skins, bones, preserved young, photographs, and film. Those records help correct old errors, but no confirmed evidence moves the species beyond nineteen thirty-six. Its official status is Extinct.
scenes.imagePrompt: Cinematic wildlife documentary reconstruction of a scientifically plausible adult thylacine with a lean long-backed dog-like body, sandy yellow-brown short coat, about fifteen dark brown stripes across the lower back and rump, stiff tapering tail aligned with the spine, long narrow head, rounded upright ears and slim legs; historically reconstructed living animal seen in the lit frame of a 1930s black-and-white film projection inside a dark museum study room, preserved skull and flat skin resting respectfully on a wooden table below, camera over the researcher's shoulder with no person visible, evidence-focused composition, warm projector beam and deep shadows, photorealistic, historically appropriate objects, subtle film grain, restrained natural color grading, high detail, no readable text, no watermark, no logo, no blood, 16:9 widescreen composition
scenes.factNote: Later sightings remain unverified; the species is listed Extinct under Australian and Tasmanian law and by the IUCN.

scenes.title: A Warning That Remains
scenes.subtitle: 7 September: a day to act
scenes.narration: The thylacine survives as a symbol because its loss was recent, recorded, and avoidable. In Australia, September seventh is now National Threatened Species Day. The date asks a hard question. Which animals are being blamed, ignored, or protected too late today? Extinction is final. The chance to prevent it is not.
scenes.imagePrompt: Cinematic wildlife documentary reconstruction of a scientifically plausible adult thylacine with a lean long-backed dog-like body, sandy yellow-brown short coat, about fifteen dark brown stripes across the lower back and rump, stiff tapering tail aligned with the spine, long narrow head, rounded upright ears and slim legs; living animal on the left walking into a restored Tasmanian grassland and open forest at sunrise, wide low camera view, gold light catching the stripes while the empty landscape opens ahead, hopeful but elegiac final image, photorealistic, realistic anatomy, historically appropriate natural environment, subtle film grain, restrained natural color grading, high detail, no text, no watermark, no logo, no collar, no modern objects, no blood, 16:9 widescreen composition
scenes.factNote: Australia marks National Threatened Species Day on 7 September, the date the last known thylacine died.

outro.title: The Thylacine's Warning
outro.subtitle: Extinct — protection came too late
outro.narration: The thylacine was not a monster, and it was not saved by good intentions that came at the end. Its last days show why action must begin while a species still has time. Next, we travel north for Steller's sea cow, a giant lost in only twenty-seven years. Subscribe for the next story.
